checks.a_analysis: The output faithfully represents the text found in the source document. Section headers like 'Attendees' and 'Meeting Proceedings' were added for structure, which is an acceptable AI addition. No hallucinated body text or invented paragraphs were found.
There is no contact information (phone numbers, emails, URLs) in the source document, and none were fabricated in the output.

All sections from the single-page source document (Call to Order, Roll Call, Senate Bill 600 discussion, and Adjournment) along with the attendee lists are present in the output. The informational content of the footer text is captured in the metadata and red stamp text, so its omission as a body element is not a defect.
There are no formal tables in the source document. The attendee lists are correctly converted into structured paragraphs/lists.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: All substantive text within each section has been preserved. Section III, which contains two paragraphs in the source, is fully represented across two elements in the JSON output.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The output contains a minor correction of a typo found in the source (changing 'Chariman' to 'Chairman' in Section I). This is considered acceptable normalization that preserves meaning. All other text, including dates, names, and times, matches the source.

checks.a_analysis: The output correctly captures all text content from the source PDF without duplication. Elements such as the red approval stamp and the header/footer text are included once in the logical document structure.
The output uses H2 for major headings. While it doesn't use an H1, the hierarchy is flat and consistent. There are no skipped levels (e.g., H1 to H3).
There are no tables in the source document. The attendee lists are correctly represented as text blocks/paragraphs rather than being forced into a table structure.

The reading order follows the source document logically, moving from the title/meta information to the attendee lists and then the meeting minutes. The side-by-side columns for 'Members' and 'Staff' are linearized appropriately.

The ordered list for meeting proceedings (I-IV) is broken in the output. Items I, II, and the first half of III are grouped in one list. The second half of item III ('Mr. Greene made the motion...') is extracted as a standalone paragraph. Item IV ('Adjourn...') is then placed in a second, separate ordered list. This breaks the structural continuity and numbering of the proceedings.
checks.b_evidence_source:
III. Senate Bill 600... [paragraph 2] Mr. Greene made the motion to approve the amended proposed language as presented...
IV. Adjourn – The meeting was adjourned at 6:18 PM.
checks.b_evidence_output:
[First list contains items I, II, III (part 1)]
"type": "paragraph", "content": [ { "text": "Mr. Greene made the motion to approve the amended proposed language as presented..." } ]
[Second list containing item IV]

checks.a_analysis: No links were present in the source PDF and none were added to the output.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output maintains the logical organization of the source PDF. The document header information (title, date, location), attendee lists, and meeting proceedings are all presented in the same relative sequence. The conversion from a two-column name list to single-column lists is an expected responsive design choice and does not negatively impact the visual flow or understanding of the document. The watermark text from the PDF is appropriately relocated as text at the top of the HTML document.

There are no formal tables (with borders or distinct grid structures) in the source PDF. The attendee lists, while organized in columns in the PDF, have been correctly rendered as text lists in the HTML, which is an appropriate visual representation for this type of content.

The meaningful images in the document are the NC DIT 911 Board logos. These logos are correctly present in both the top and bottom of the rendered HTML output, mirroring their placement in the source PDF.

There is no visual duplication of body content in the HTML output. The repetition of the document title at the very top and as a main heading is an intentional accessible design feature as noted in the instructions, and not a defect.
